focus: Resolve seam continuity with minimal edits.
next_head_excerpt:
# Chapter 13

From the eastern side of the char came, now and then, the low collapsing sound of bank giving way somewhere out of sight, the scrape and slump of a huge wet mat dragged over earth. Halima heard it crossing the yard to the schoolroom. Inside, the questions Nura had gone home with were already waiting on the blackboard in four chalk headings: NAME, RELATION, YEARS IN HOUSE, WITNESSES. Aminul had pushed the children's benches into two hard ranks beneath them. The room smelled of old dust, damp jute, and the sour chalk children sucked when the teacher's back was turned. There had been no rain since yesterday, but the river still made itself known.

Nura sat at the teacher's table with ruled paper stacked by her elbow, the small burn mark on her wrist showing each time she dipped her pen. She had tied her hair back too tight. Since the trip to the neighboring char she had moved with a colder economy. A day spent looking for a father had burned out whatever use she still had for softness. She did not look up when Halima came in. She only said, "He's made groups," and slid her chin toward Aminul.

Aminul stood near the board with his folders held a little off his shirtfront to save them from sweat. Even in this heat he had managed a pressed collar. Chalk dust sat on one cuff. "The clerk sent word from the mainland," he said. "The magistrate may come the day after tomorrow. If not then, the next morning. We don't have room for confusion now."

Halima put her palm on the nearest bench. The wood still held the sticky warmth of children. "A house isn't an exam answer," she said.

"No," Aminul said. "But if five people from one yard give me five versions, the affidavit will tear in the first question."

He had arranged the first pile already, with weak papers on top a...
prev_tail_excerpt:
...he lane again before anyone else did, stopping one step short of the veranda. The thimble from her scarf was in her fist now instead of hidden at her neck.

"If I stand there," she asked, looking not at Halima but at Rahima's name on the page, "they'll ask about the months before my elder girl's wedding. All of it."

"Yes," Halima said.

Hasina pressed the thimble into her palm until the skin around her knuckles blanched. "Not today," she said, and turned away toward the west lane. She went home.

Across the schoolyard, near the path to the eastern ground, Mofiz had formed his own small queue without ever calling it one. Men stood with him holding document sleeves and tax receipts, their bodies arranged in a curve of caution around his white clothes. Jamila and Majed were there now. Majed held the papers. Jamila held herself.

Aminul said quietly, "If the magistrate asks later who encouraged which route, I'll have to answer truthfully."

"Then answer it," Halima said.

Across the yard, Mofiz tilted his head toward Jamila's sleeve. "You'd do better not to invite wind into a house that's still standing."

Jamila's answer carried because the yard had emptied enough for small sounds to travel. "My house isn't settled. It's only standing. Put us with the papers we have."

Majed handed Mofiz the sleeve.

Rahima stood at Halima's shoulder with the goat rope looped around her wrist. Nura's page lay open under Halima's palm, Rahima's name written first among the people willing to be witnessed into existence. Nura kept one hand on the blue folder, over the inside flap where the neighboring char's name sat in pencil. By dark the tea stalls would turn the whole schoolyard into house list and blood list. The names were stupid. The split was not. Across the yard, Jamila kept walking.
